agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to replace 1965 Zoning Bylaw with new 2026 version
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to repeal Burnaby's current Zoning Bylaw (from 1965) and replace it with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would also introduce new Development Permit Area Guidelines and reassign some properties to different zoning districts. If approved, this would change the rules for what you can build and how you can use your property in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak up about how the new rules would affect your property.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
BURNABY ZONING BYLAW - PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT
Purpose: To seek Council approval to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $2M Year 2 operating funding for Burnaby Housing Authority
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving $2 million in Year 2 operating funding for the Burnaby Housing Authority (BHA). This money would help the BHA run its housing programs and services for another year. The decision has not been made yet — council will vote on this request at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
BHA - ASSISTANCE REQUEST FOR YEAR 2 OPERATING FUNDING
307
Purpose: To seek Council approval of the Burnaby Housing Authority
Corporation’s assistance request for Year 2 Operating Funding.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Housing Authority Year 2 operating funding request
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving the Burnaby Housing Authority's request for Year 2 operating funding. This is a proposal to provide money to help the Authority run its housing programs for another year. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
BHA - ASSISTANCE REQUEST FOR YEAR 2 OPERATING FUNDING
Purpose: To seek Council approval of the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s assistance request for Year 2 Operating Funding.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on 2026 property tax rates for Burnaby businesses
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on proposed property tax rates for 2026. This includes municipal tax rates (the portion that goes to the City of Burnaby), regional tax rates (the portion that goes to Metro Vancouver), and Business Improvement Area (BIA) tax rates (an extra tax paid by businesses in certain areas to fund local improvements like street cleaning or marketing). If approved, these rates will determine how much property tax you pay in 2026 based on your property's assessed value. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider and vote on the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, these rates will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
2026 PROPERTY TAX RATES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for 2026 municipal, regional and business improvement area (BIA) property tax rates.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 property tax rates for municipal, regional, and BIA
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on the proposed 2026 property tax rates for municipal, regional, and Business Improvement Area (BIA) properties. A BIA is a local area where businesses pay an extra tax to fund improvements and marketing for that district. If approved, these rates will determine how much property tax you pay next year for your business property, including the portion that goes to the regional government (Metro Vancouver) and your local BIA.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
2026 PROPERTY TAX RATES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for 2026 municipal, regional and business improvement area (BIA) property tax rates.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to appoint Mark Norton as Approving Officer for subdivisions
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to appoint Mark Norton as the Approving Officer for subdivision applications. An Approving Officer is the person who reviews and approves or denies requests to split a piece of land into smaller lots (subdivisions). This decision would affect anyone planning to subdivide land in Burnaby, as the officer has authority over the approval process. The appointment has not yet been approved — Council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
APPOINTMENT OF APPROVING OFFICER
322
Purpose: To seek Council approval to appoint an Approving Officer.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to appoint an Approving Officer
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to appoint an Approving Officer. An Approving Officer is a person who has the legal authority to approve plans for new subdivisions (splitting land into smaller lots) and road closures. This is a routine administrative appointment, but it affects anyone who wants to subdivide land or close a road in Burnaby. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider and vote on the appointment at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you right now. Watch for the council decision — this appointment affects future subdivision and road-closure approvals.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
APPOINTMENT OF APPROVING OFFICER
Purpose: To seek Council approval to appoint an Approving Officer.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to appoint an Approving Officer for land matters
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to appoint an Approving Officer. This officer is a person who has the legal authority to approve or reject plans for things like subdivisions (splitting a piece of land into smaller lots), road layouts, and other land development projects. If appointed, this officer would make decisions on these applications, which could affect property owners, developers, and anyone planning to build or divide land in Burnaby. The decision is not final yet — Council will consider and vote on the appointment at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — if you have a pending land development or subdivision application, this appointment could affect who reviews it.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
APPOINTMENT OF APPROVING OFFICER
Purpose: To seek Council approval to appoint an Approving Officer.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Retaining wall height variances at 8012 11th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) for a property at 8012 11th Avenue. A DVP is a special permission that allows a property owner to build something that doesn't quite follow the usual rules. In this case, the owner wants to build retaining walls that are taller than what the current zoning bylaw normally allows. Council will decide whether to approve this variance at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own property near 8012 11th Avenue, watch for the council decision — it could affect your property's drainage or stability.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
DVP #26-01 - 8012 11TH AVENUE - RETAINING WALLS HEIGHT
VARIANCES
324
Purpose: To seek Council approval for Development Variance Permit
(DVP) #26-01.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on retaining wall height variance at 8012 11th Ave
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) for a property at 8012 11th Avenue. A DVP is a special permission that lets a property owner break certain rules in the city's zoning bylaw. In this case, the owner wants to build retaining walls that are taller than what the current rules allow. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or are developing property near 8012 11th Avenue, watch for the council meeting date and consider attending to voice any concerns about the proposed retaining wall height.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
DVP #26-01 - 8012 11TH AVENUE - RETAINING WALLS HEIGHT VARIANCES
Purpose: To seek Council approval for Development Variance Permit (DVP) #26-01.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to vote on $4M insurance renewal for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on approving $4,013,855 for the city's annual property and liability insurance renewals for 2026. This covers insurance that protects the city and its operations from financial losses due to property damage or lawsuits. If approved, the contracts will be awarded to insurance providers for the coming year.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
2026 INSURANCE PROGRAMS - CONTRACT RENEWALS
337
Purpose: To request approval to award insurance contracts for the
annual property and liability insurance programs.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to award insurance contracts for property and liability programs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving the renewal of contracts for the city's annual property and liability insurance programs. This is a routine business decision to ensure the city has proper insurance coverage for its buildings, equipment, and operations. If approved, the contracts would take effect for the 2026 policy year. This does not directly change any rules or fees for local businesses.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
2026 INSURANCE PROGRAMS - CONTRACT RENEWALS
Purpose: To request approval to award insurance contracts for the annual property and liability insurance programs.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to approve $4,000 for Heritage Commission conference trip
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to spend up to $4,000 so members of Burnaby's Community Heritage Commission (CHC) can attend the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC from May 4-6, 2026. This is not yet approved — the decision is up for a vote at an upcoming council meeting.

key_dates.label: Heritage BC Conference — proposed attendance dates
Heritage BC Conference — proposed attendance ends

2026 HERITAGE BC CONFERENCE
342
Purpose: To seek Council approval for members of the Community
Heritage Commission (CHC) to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC
Conference in Squamish, BC from May 4-6, 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to approve Heritage Commission members attending 2026 BC conference
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to allow members of Burnaby's Community Heritage Commission (CHC) to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC, from May 4-6, 2026. The CHC advises Council on heritage matters, and this trip would let members learn from other communities and bring back ideas. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider whether to approve the travel and related costs at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to approve Heritage Commission trip to 2026 BC conference
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to allow members of Burnaby's Community Heritage Commission (CHC) to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC, from May 4-6, 2026. If approved, this would let commission members travel to the conference to learn about heritage conservation and network with other communities. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider this at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to award $1M+ contract for networking software licenses
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding a $1,047,545 contract to Insight Canada for the supply and delivery of networking software licenses. This is a proposed contract award — it has not yet been approved. If approved, the city would purchase software licenses needed to run its computer network systems. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CA - NETWORKING SOFTWARE LICENSES
344
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the
supply and delivery of networking software licenses.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract award for networking software licenses
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of networking software licenses. If approved, this would authorize the city to purchase the software needed to run its computer networks. This is a routine procurement decision that keeps city technology systems working.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CA - NETWORKING SOFTWARE LICENSES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of networking software licenses.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $1.96M contract for civic office furniture
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding a $1,962,600 contract to Heritage Office Furnishings for the supply, delivery and installation of furniture for new civic facilities. This is part of the fit-out of new city offices and public spaces. The decision has not been made yet — it will be considered at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
CA - NEW CIVIC FACILITY FIT-OUT, FURNISHINGS AND
EQUIPMENT
346
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the
supply, delivery and installation of office and facility furniture.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for new civic facility furniture
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply, delivery, and installation of office and facility furniture for a new civic facility. If approved, this contract would cover all the furniture and equipment needed to fit out the new building. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $47,000 KPMG audit contract increase
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change that would add $47,000 to the amount paid to KPMG for external audit services. An external audit is an independent check of the city's financial records to make sure they are accurate and follow the rules. This is not a final decision yet — Council will consider and vote on the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.10
CC - EXTERNAL AUDIT SERVICES
349
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for
External Audit Services.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract change for external audit services
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed change to the city's contract for external audit services. This is not a final decision yet. If approved, the change would modify the existing agreement with the company that audits the city's financial records. The purpose is to ensure the city's finances are reviewed properly. The specific details of the contract change are on the council agenda for discussion and a vote.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.10
CC - EXTERNAL AUDIT SERVICES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for External Audit Services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $384,893 contract change for Burnaby Lake geotechnical work
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change (an amendment to an existing agreement) that would add $384,893 to the geotechnical consulting contract with WSP for the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex project. Geotechnical services involve studying soil and ground conditions to ensure buildings are safe and stable. If approved, this would increase the total contract value. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider and vote on this at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you — this is an internal city contract decision. Watch for the council vote result if you work on this project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to vote on protected intersections for safer streets
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. If approved, the new rules would require 'protected intersection geometry' — a safer design that uses curbs and barriers to separate pedestrians and cyclists from turning vehicles at intersections. The goal is to improve road safety and make it easier for people of all ages and abilities to walk and bike in the town centre. This is not yet law; council will decide at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new design standards could affect future construction or renovation projects in Burnaby's town centre.

and-abilities mobility.

The change would require new intersections to use 'protected intersection geometry' — a safer design that separates pedestrians and cyclists from turning vehicles using curbs and barriers. This is meant to make streets safer for people walking and biking, and to support travel options that work for all ages and abilities. The decision has not been made yet; Council will vote on whether to endorse the amendment at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Brentwood Park homes may be exempt from BC SSMUH rules via building bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal that would exempt certain properties in Brentwood Park from the Province's new Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) requirements. These properties have a 'restrictive covenant' — a legal promise on the land title limiting what can be built. The proposal would amend the Burnaby Building Bylaw city-wide to require compliance with restrictive covenants and statutory building schemes as part of building permit approval, affecting residential, commercial, and industrial properties. Staff do not recommend this approach due to added costs and process delays.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on April 28, 2026 — this decision could affect whether you can build additional units on your Brentwood Park property.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Brentwood Park SSMUH exemption proposal

BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION - RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS
AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES
463
Purpose: To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding
3

exempting Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from
Provincial SSMUH requirements.

attachments.title: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schem

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on exempting Brentwood Park from BC housing rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a motion that would exempt certain properties in Brentwood Park from new provincial rules that allow more housing units on single-family lots. The provincial rules, called SSMUH (Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing), are meant to let owners build things like duplexes or triplexes on land that was previously only for single homes. Some Brentwood Park properties have 'restrictive covenants' — legal promises written into the land title that limit what can be built. Council will decide whether to let those covenants override the new provincial rules, which could stop some owners from adding extra housing units. This is a proposed decision and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, it could limit your ability to build additional units on your Brentwood Park property.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION - RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES
Purpose: To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial SSMUH requirements.

attachments.title: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schem

agenda_briefs.title: Burnaby staff recommend against exempting Brentwood Park from SSMUH rules
agenda_briefs.summary: A staff report recommends Council not exempt Brentwood Park properties with restrictive covenants from provincial Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) requirements. The City is bound by provincial law to zone for SSMUH, and restrictive covenants are private property interests not recognized as exemptions. Staff propose instead a City-wide Building Bylaw amendment requiring compliance with such covenants at building permit stage, but warn this would limit suites and duplexes, increase application costs and processing times, and affect properties citywide including commercial and industrial sites.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's decision on the staff recommendation at the April 28, 2026 meeting.

key_dates.label: Council meeting on Brentwood Park SSMUH exemption staff report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 3900 Grand Promenade to add hotel, restaurant, and 362 more homes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would change the approved development at 3900 Grand Promenade and nearby addresses. The developer wants to replace some planned office space with a hotel and restaurants, add 362 more market strata (privately owned) residential units, and make two towers taller — Tower 1B by seven storeys and Tower 1A by four storeys. The proposal also shifts the required affordable housing contribution for Phase 1 to a different site (the Carrigan site). This is a change from the previous rezoning approval (REZ #18-32) that was finalized in September 2024. The decision is not final — council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — you may want to attend or submit comments on this rezoning proposal.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14808 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No.
7, 2026 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin
Rd., 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place)
681
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands
pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received
4

Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two
market strata residential buildings of 80 (Tower 1B) and 73
(Tower 1A) storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail
and office podium. As part of the subject amendment rezoning
application, the applicant is proposing to replace a portion of
the commercial office use with hotel and restaurant uses, and
add 362 additional market strata units from the development
concept approved under REZ #18-32, resulting in an additional
seven storeys for Tower 1B and four storeys for Tower 1A. In
addition, as part of the subject amendment rezoning
application, the applicant is proposing to provide the
inclusionary requirement associated with Phase 1 only, to be
transferred to the Carrigan site, which is an amendment from
the previous rezoning approval.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on rezoning for hotel, more homes at 3900 Grand Promenade
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning amendment for four properties near the Carrigan site. The developer wants to replace some planned office space with a hotel and restaurants, and add 362 more market strata condo units. This would make Tower 1B seven storeys taller and Tower 1A four storeys taller than previously approved. The developer also proposes to shift the required affordable housing for Phase 1 to the Carrigan site instead of building it on these properties. This is a proposed change to a rezoning that was already approved in 2024 — it has not been decided yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit a written comment to Burnaby City Council before the vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14808 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 7, 2026 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Rd., 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 (Tower 1B) and 73 (Tower 1A) storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. As part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to replace a portion of the commercial office use with hotel and restaurant uses, and add 362 additional market strata units from the development concept approved under REZ #18-32, resulting in an additional seven storeys for Tower 1B and four storeys for Tower 1A. In addition, as part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to provide the inclusionary requirement associated with Phase 1 only, to be transferred to the Carrigan site, which is an amendment from the previous rezoning approval.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 3900 Grand Promenade for hotel, restaurant, and 362 more homes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed bylaw amendment to rezone 3900 Grand Promenade and nearby addresses. The developer wants to swap some planned office space for a hotel and restaurant, and add 362 more market strata units (condos you can buy). This would make Tower 1B seven storeys taller and Tower 1A four storeys taller than what was already approved. The developer also wants to move some required affordable housing to a different site (the Carrigan site). This is a proposal — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on the proposed changes.

11.1.1
#14808 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 7, 2026 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Rd., 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 (Tower 1B) and 73 (Tower 1A) storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. As part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to replace a portion of the commercial office use with hotel and restaurant uses, and add 362 additional market strata units from the development concept approved under REZ #18-32, resulting in an additional seven storeys for Tower 1B and four storeys for Tower 1A. In addition, as part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to provide the inclusionary requirement associated with Phase 1 only, to be transferred to the Carrigan site, which is an amendment from the previous rezoning approval.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 12-storey mixed-use tower at 6535 Kingsway, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a rezoning application for 6535 Kingsway. A proposed bylaw would allow a 12-storey building with 290 residential units, ground-floor shops facing Kingsway, and underground parking. The project matches the city's Official Community Plan (the long-term vision for growth) and the Edmonds Community Plan. This is not yet approved — Council will decide at a future meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — you can speak for or against this proposal.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14809 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 8, 2026 - REZ #25-09 (6535 Kingsway)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a 12-storey mixed-use multiple-family development with a double-height commercial storey at street level comprised of 290 residential units, ground-oriented commercial uses fronting Kingsway, and underground parking. The proposal is consistent with the applicable OCP and the Edmonds Community Plan designations for the site.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Five-storey self-storage building at 2757 Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed bylaw amendment to rezone 2757 Norland Avenue. If approved, the change would allow construction of a five-storey enclosed self-storage building. The proposal follows the 1998 Official Community Plan policies and transition rules. This is not yet approved — the decision is up for a vote at a future council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date and attend to voice your support or concerns about the proposed self-storage building.

#14810 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 9, 2026 - REZ #25-21 (2757 Norland Avenue)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a five-storey enclosed self-storage building. The proposal is being advanced in accordance with the applicable 1998 OCP policies and approved transitionary provisions.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter rules up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed changes to Burnaby's Street and Traffic Bylaw that would update rules for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. The proposed bylaw, called 'Bylaw No. 1, 2026', would amend the existing 1964 Street and Traffic Bylaw to create a pilot program for these devices. If approved, this could change where and how e-bikes and e-scooters can be ridden on Burnaby streets and sidewalks. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new rules could affect how you operate your business if you use e-bikes or e-scooters for deliveries or services.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

690
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-
bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

If approved, this would change the rules for where and how these devices can be used on city streets. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming proposal on the council agenda.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Parks e-bike and e-scooter pilot program rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed changes to the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in city parks as part of a pilot program. The proposed bylaw amendment would create new rules for where and how these devices can be used in parks. This is not yet law — council will give first, second, and third readings to the amendment before it can take effect.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and any public hearing dates — this pilot could affect how you use e-bikes or e-scooters in Burnaby parks.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain Burnaby parks as part of a pilot program. The amendment to the Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 would create new rules for where and how these devices can be used. This is not yet law — it is up for approval at a future council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendments for e-bike and e-scooter enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed changes to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. These changes would update how the city enforces rules for e-bikes and electric kick scooters as part of a pilot program. If approved, the amendments would give the city new tools to issue tickets and enforce regulations for these vehicles. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new enforcement rules for e-bikes and e-scooters could affect your business operations or delivery services.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026 (e-bike and electric kick
scooter programs)
697
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement
Bylaw 2009 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment for e-bike and e-scooter pilot enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update how the city enforces rules for e-bikes and electric kick scooters as part of a pilot program. The amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 would allow the city to issue tickets and fines for things like improper parking, riding on sidewalks, or not following traffic rules. This is not yet law — it is up for approval at a future council meeting. If passed, the new rules would apply to anyone using an e-bike or e-scooter in Burnaby during the pilot program.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, e-bike and e-scooter riders must follow new enforcement rules.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to enforce e-bike and e-scooter pilot rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update how the city enforces rules for e-bikes and electric kick scooters during a pilot program. The amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw would allow the city to issue tickets and fines for things like riding on sidewalks, speeding, or parking illegally. This is not yet law — it is up for approval at a future council meeting. If passed, the new enforcement rules would apply to anyone using an e-bike or e-scooter in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, e-bike and e-scooter riders could face new fines.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 property tax rates bylaw up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would set the 2026 property tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in Burnaby. This bylaw is required under sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter (the law that gives BC municipalities their powers). If approved, it will determine how much property tax you pay next year based on your property's assessed value.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4
#14814 - Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026
698
Purpose: to establish tax rates upon all taxable land and
improvements pursuant to sections 197 and 216 of the
Community Charter

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 sets new property tax rates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed bylaw to set the 2026 tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in Burnaby. This bylaw, called the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026, is required under sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter (the main law that gives BC cities their powers). If approved, it will determine how much property tax you pay next year based on your property's assessed value. The decision has not been made yet — council is set to vote on this proposal.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on this bylaw — it will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
